steps.title: 안녕하세요!
steps.desc: Your very first Korean words. Korean greetings are about PEACE and RESPECT. 안녕 literally means 'peace.' Every greeting you learn here is a window into Korean culture.
goals: Say hello and goodbye in Korean
Learn two different goodbyes (leaving vs staying)
Understand how Korean differs from English
See how Korean verbs work at a high level

steps.note:
COMPOUND: 안녕 (peace) + 하 (do) + 세요 (please/honorific).
Literally: 'Do you have peace?'
Use with EVERYONE. It's universally polite.

steps.title: 🌏 How Korean Is Different From English
steps.text:
A few things to know before you dive in:

• Korean puts the verb LAST (you'll see this every sentence)
• Korean has NO articles (a, the). Context tells you.
• Korean verbs DON'T change for person (I go, you go, he goes: all the same)
• Korean questions sound like statements (your voice just rises)
• Korean drops subjects constantly: 'coffee exists' can mean 'I have coffee'

deepDive.title: The Big Picture: Korean vs English
deepDive.text:
SOV vs SVO:
English: I coffee drink (weird!)
Korean: 저는 커피를 마셔요 (I coffee drink: normal!)
The verb ALWAYS goes last in Korean.

Agglutinative language:
Korean snaps pieces onto verb stems.
One verb can carry tense + politeness + mood.
Think of it like LEGO for grammar.

Context-heavy:
Korean trusts you to figure out who and what from context.
No grammatical gender. No plural markers (usually). No articles.

GOOD NEWS:
No irregular spelling. Consistent pronunciation rules.
Every syllable block is pronounced. What you see is what you say.

steps.title: 📖 Korean Verbs End in -다
steps.text:
Every Korean verb in the dictionary ends in -다:
• 하다 = to do
• 가다 = to go
• 먹다 = to eat
• 있다 = to exist

To USE a verb, you drop -다 and add an ending:
하다 → 하 + 세요 = 하세요 (please do)

The ending shows politeness, tense, and mood.
You're learning the POLITE ending (-요) from day one.

deepDive.title: Why -다 Matters
deepDive.text:
Dictionary form: always ends in -다.

Stem: everything BEFORE -다.
• 하다 → stem is 하
• 가다 → stem is 가
• 먹다 → stem is 먹
• 있다 → stem is 있

Endings snap onto the stem:
• -세요: polite request
• -이에요/예요: am/is (you'll learn this in Lesson 2)
• -아/어요: polite present tense (coming in Unit 2)

You'll eventually stack multiple endings.
For now: -다 is dictionary form, -요 means polite.

steps.q: 안녕하세요 literally means:
steps.hint: 안녕 (peace) + 하세요 (do you have / please do)

steps.note:
Said to someone who is LEAVING.
You stay, they go → 가세요 (please go).
안녕히: peacefully.
You are wishing THEM a peaceful journey.

Said to someone who STAYS.
You leave, they stay → 계세요 (please stay).
You are wishing THEM a peaceful stay.
Two goodbyes: one for the leaver, one for the stayer.

Casual hello AND goodbye in one word.
Only use with CLOSE friends your age or younger.
⚠️ NEVER use with strangers, elders, or at work.
Stay with 안녕하세요 until you know when 안녕 is safe to use.

steps.title: 🧠 Goodbye Summary: Formal vs Casual
steps.text:
Korean has TWO formal goodbyes and ONE casual:

🚶 They leave, you stay → 안녕히 가세요 (go in peace)
🏠 You leave, they stay → 안녕히 계세요 (stay in peace)
😊 Close friends only → 안녕 (casual hi AND bye)

Both formal goodbyes use 안녕히 (peacefully). The verb changes:
• 가세요 = please go
• 계세요 = please stay

Think about what the OTHER person is doing.
That tells you which formal goodbye to use.

steps.title: 🙇 Bowing Basics
steps.text:
Korean greetings usually come with a bow.

• Slight nod (15°): friends, casual settings
• Medium bow (30°): normal polite greeting
• Deep bow (45°+): elders, formal situations, apologies

When you say 안녕하세요, a small bow shows respect.
You don't need to bow deeply in casual settings.
Just a gentle nod of the head is enough.

steps.q: You're leaving a shop. The shopkeeper stays. You say:
steps.hint: THEY stay, so you wish them to 'stay in peace'

steps.q: Your friend is leaving the café. You stay. You say:
steps.hint: THEY go, so you wish them to 'go in peace'

steps.s: You leave the office. Your coworker stays. You say: ___
steps.hint: Your coworker STAYS. Wish them to 'stay in peace.'

steps.q: 가세요 in 안녕히 가세요 means 'please go.' You say this to someone who is:
steps.hint: 가 = go. You wish the LEAVER to go in peace.

steps.q: 안녕 (casual) is appropriate when talking to:
steps.hint: 안녕 is casual. Only safe with close friends your age or younger.

steps.s: A student greets their teacher in the morning: ___
steps.hint: A teacher is senior. Use the polite, universal greeting.

steps.q: In the dictionary, Korean verbs always end in:
steps.hint: You learned this in the tip card. Dictionary form always ends in this.

steps.s: Two friends part ways on the street. Both are leaving. They both say: ___
steps.hint: Both are GOING. Each wishes the other to 'go in peace.'

steps.q: In Korean sentences, the verb goes:
steps.hint: Korean is a verb-last language. Subject first, verb last.

pairs.nl: Meeting someone for the first time

steps.q: You visit a Korean home. The host greets you with 안녕하세요. After tea, you leave. The host stays. What do you say as you go?
steps.hint: You are LEAVING, the host STAYS. Wish them to stay in peace.

steps.title: I Am ___
steps.desc: Introducing yourself in Korean. You'll learn the most basic sentence pattern: '저는 [name]이에요.' Notice where the verb goes: at the END.

steps.note:
Polite 'I'. Use with everyone.
Casual 'I' (나) taught later in Unit 10.
Always use 저 until you learn when 나 is safe.

steps.exampleEn: As for me, I am a student.
steps.note:
Marks 'what we're talking about.'
는 after a vowel: 저는 (저 ends in ㅓ).
은 after a consonant: 학생은 (학생 ends in ㅇ batchim).
Don't overthink it yet. Just copy the pattern.

steps.title: 🎀 You're Speaking Politely (해요체)
steps.text:
Everything you're learning uses POLITE speech (해요체).

• You can tell by the -요 at the end of every sentence
• Korean has MULTIPLE speech levels (7 total!)
• 해요체 is the safe default. Use it with everyone.
• You'll learn other levels later
• For now: -요 at the end = polite. Always.

deepDive.title: Why Politeness Is Built Into Korean Grammar
deepDive.text:
English uses WORDS for politeness:
'Can you please pass the salt?'

Korean uses VERB ENDINGS (grammar, not vocabulary).
Same sentence, different ending = different respect level.

Age determines EVERYTHING:
Who speaks first, which ending you use.
This is why Koreans ask your age immediately.
It's not rude. It's NECESSARY to know which
verb endings to use with you.

해요체 (-요 ending): safe with literally everyone.
반말 (no -요): friends your age, younger people, NEVER strangers.

steps.note:
이에요 after consonant (ends in consonant):
학생이에요.
→ I am a student.

예요 after vowel (ends in vowel):
엠마예요.
→ I am Emma.

The verb goes at the END. Always. -요 = polite marker.

steps.title: 🔍 SOV: Verb Goes Last
steps.text:
Every sentence you've seen so far:

저는 사라예요. (I am Sara.)
저는 학생이에요. (I am a student.)

Subject, then VERB (last).

This is how Korean works. The verb ALWAYS goes at the end.
You'll see this in every single lesson.
We'll formally name this pattern in Unit 3.

Bonus: 저는 is often dropped in casual speech.
사라예요 alone means 'I'm Sara' from context.

steps.q: 저는 사라___. (Sara)
steps.hint: 사라 ends in a VOWEL (ㅏ). Vowel → 예요

steps.q: 저는 학생___.
steps.hint: 학생 ends in a CONSONANT (ㅇ batchim). Consonant → 이에요

steps.q: 저는 민수___. (Minsu)
steps.hint: 민수 ends in a VOWEL (ㅜ). Vowel → 예요
사라 ends in ㅏ (vowel). Vowel → 예요
선생님 ends in ㅁ batchim (consonant). Consonant → 이에요

steps.q: The -요 at the end of 이에요 shows that you are being:
steps.hint: 해요체: the -요 ending = polite speech level

steps.q: Which marker goes after 저 to mean 'as for me'?
steps.hint: 저 ends in a vowel (ㅓ). Vowel → 는

steps.q: In casual speech, Koreans often drop 저는. So '사라예요' by itself means:
steps.hint: When 저는 is dropped, context tells you who is speaking.

steps.q: In Korean, the verb goes:
steps.hint: Korean is SOV. Verb last, always.
Polite 'I' is 저. 나 is casual (taught later).

steps.q: Korean has how many speech levels?
steps.hint: You learned this in the 해요체 tip card!

steps.q: 저는 엠마예요. Why 예요 and not 이에요?
steps.hint: After vowel → 예요. After consonant → 이에요.

steps.q: To introduce yourself at a Korean office, you use the pattern: 안녕하세요, 저는 [name]...
opts: 이에요 or 예요 (depending on last sound)
steps.ans: 이에요 or 예요 (depending on last sound)
steps.hint: 저는 + name + 이에요/예요. Consonant → 이에요, vowel → 예요

steps.title: How Korean Verbs Actually Work
steps.desc: You've said 이에요, 가세요, 있어요. But how are they built? This lesson shows you the system behind every Korean verb you'll ever use. Learn this once. Use it forever.
goals: See how every Korean verb is built from two parts
Recognise the 하다 verb family that unlocks hundreds of words

steps.note:
STEP 1: Every Korean verb dictionary form ends in -다.
STEP 2: Strip -다 → you get the STEM.
가다 → stem: 가
STEP 3: Add the ending.
가 + 요 = 가요 ✓

The stem 가 carries the meaning.
-요 makes it polite.
This is the formula for EVERY Korean verb.

먹다 → stem: 먹
먹 + 어요 = 먹어요 ✓

Why -어요 and not -요?
Because 먹 ends in a consonant — it needs a connecting vowel.

⚡ JRTF: Why -어요 here but -아요 elsewhere?
There's a rule. You'll see it in Unit 2. For now: just notice that -어 appears between consonant stems and -요.

하다 is the MOST important Korean verb.
하 + 아요 → 해요 (하 + 아 contracts to 해)

하다 snaps onto nouns to create verb phrases:
공부 (study) + 하다 = 공부하다 → 공부해요
운동 (exercise) + 하다 = 운동하다 → 운동해요
일 (work) + 하다 = 일하다 → 일해요
요리 (cooking) + 하다 = 요리하다 → 요리해요

⚡ LEGO: Every noun + 하다 = verb. This single pattern unlocks hundreds of words.

steps.title: 🚂 The Train Metaphor
steps.text:
Think of every Korean verb as a TRAIN:

🚂 STEM = the engine (carries the meaning)
🚃 ENDING = the car that snaps on

가 + 요 = 가요 (go, politely)
먹 + 어요 = 먹어요 (eat, politely)
공부하 + 여요 → 공부해요 (study, politely)

Right now you only have ONE car: -요 (the politeness car).
Soon you'll add more cars:
🚃 Past tense car: -었/았어요
🚃 Future car: -(으)ㄹ 거예요
🚃 Request car: -(으)세요

Same engine. Different cars. That's Korean.

deepDive.title: Why This Is Better Than Memorising
deepDive.text:
Without the engine:
먹어요 = memorised chunk
가요 = memorised chunk
해요 = memorised chunk

With the engine:
STEM + ENDING = infinite verbs

You just saw 하다 attaches to nouns.
That means:
산책하다 (take a walk) → 산책해요
전화하다 (phone someone) → 전화해요
사랑하다 (to love) → 사랑해요

You can now form verbs you've NEVER SEEN.
That's the difference between memorising and generating.

steps.q: What is the stem of 먹다?
steps.hint: Strip -다 from the dictionary form. What remains is the stem.

steps.q: What is the stem of 가다?
하다 → 해요. The -요 signals:
steps.hint: -요 at the end = polite speech (해요체). You're in this register from day one.

steps.q: 공부 (study) + 하다 = ?
steps.hint: 하다 → 해요. Attach to noun: 공부 + 해요 = 공부해요. I study.

steps.exampleEn: Is there coffee? / Do you have coffee?
steps.note:
있다 → stem: 있 (ends in consonant)
있 + 어요 = 있어요 ✓

없다 → stem: 없
없 + 어요 = 없어요 ✓

Same pattern: consonant stem + 어요.

steps.q: 있다 → stem is:
steps.hint: Strip -다 from 있다. The stem is 있.

steps.q: Which of these is NOT a 하다 verb?
steps.hint: 가다 = to go. It is its own verb. 공부/운동/요리 all attach 하다.

steps.title: 📋 The Three Patterns You've Seen
steps.text:
Every verb you've used so far fits one of these:

① VOWEL STEM + 요
가다 → 가 + 요 = 가요
(stem ends in a vowel, no connector needed)

② CONSONANT STEM + 어요
먹다 → 먹 + 어요 = 먹어요
있다 → 있 + 어요 = 있어요
(consonant stem needs -어 between stem and 요)

③ 하다 → 해요 (always, no exceptions)
공부하다 → 공부해요

⚡ Why does ② use -어요 and not -아요?
That rule has a name. You'll learn it in Unit 2.

steps.hint: I go to school. I eat there. I also study (공부해요).

steps.q: You want to say 'I exercise' (운동). Which is correct?
steps.hint: 운동 + 하다 → 하다 becomes 해요. Always.

steps.q: The stem of 이에요/예요 is:
steps.hint: 이에요 = 이다(to be) → stem 이 + 에요. The copula verb follows the same stem + ending rule.

steps.title: 🎯 What You Can Now Do
steps.text:
With just STEM + ENDING you can now:

✅ Form ANY regular verb in polite present tense
✅ Understand that -요 = polite register (해요체)
✅ Generate 하다 verbs you've never seen before
✅ Analyse every verb you encounter: stem? ending? what does each part do?

This is how a polyglot thinks.
Not: 'I memorised this phrase.'
But: 'I see stem 먹, I know what it means, I know what -어요 does, I can build this form myself.'

Unit 2 gives you the rule for -아요 vs -어요.
For now: the engine is running. 🚂

steps.title: Free Vocabulary!
steps.desc: Korean borrowed hundreds of English words. They're written in Hangul but sound like English. This is FREE vocabulary! You already know these words.

steps.note:
KONGLISH: From English 'coffee'.
Just read it in Hangul: 커 (keo) + 피 (pi).
FREE vocabulary! You already know the meaning!
KONGLISH: Sounds almost identical to English.

KONGLISH: 버 sounds like 'buh', not 'bur'.
Korean doesn't have the English 'uh' sound exactly.

KONGLISH: Korean-ified pronunciation.
컴(keom) + 퓨(pyu) + 터(teo): 3 blocks.

steps.q: 커피 in English is:
How many syllable blocks does 컴퓨터 have?

steps.note: KONGLISH: Very close to English.

KONGLISH: Korea has one of the highest
café densities in the world!

KONGLISH: Five syllable blocks!
아(a)-이(i)-스(seu)-크(keu)-림(rim).

KONGLISH: Another essential word.
인(in)-터(teo)-넷(net).

steps.title: 💡 Konglish = Free Words
steps.text:
Korean has HUNDREDS of English loanwords (콩글리시 = Konglish).

커피, 택시, 버스, 호텔, 카페, 케이크, 초콜릿, 카메라, 피아노...

The catch: pronunciation is Korean-ified.
'Computer' → 컴퓨터 (keom-pyu-teo)

Read the Hangul to get the Korean pronunciation.
Don't use the English pronunciation. Koreans won't understand!

steps.q: 아이스크림 has how many syllable blocks?
Which word means 'cafe'?

steps.title: 🔊 Why Pronunciation Changes
steps.text:
Konglish words are adapted to Korean sounds:

• Korean has no 'f' sound → 커피 (keo-pi), not 'coffee'
• Korean has no 'v' sound → 서비스 (seo-bi-seu)
• Final consonants are simplified → 인터넷 (net becomes net)
• Syllables are added to break up consonant clusters → 버스 (beo-seu)

This is normal. Every language adapts loanwords to fit its sound system.

steps.q: You're in Seoul and want to find a hotel. You ask: ___이 어디예요?
Why should you NOT use English pronunciation for Konglish words?
steps.hint: Konglish uses Korean-ified sounds, not English sounds
The place where you drink coffee: 카페

steps.title: Yes, No & Thank You
steps.desc: Three survival words. Plus we'll decompose 감사합니다 into parts, just like we did with 안녕하세요.

steps.note:
Often sounds like 'de' in fast speech.
Very common. You'll hear it constantly
as a conversation filler meaning 'uh-huh, I'm listening.'

Polite 'no'.
아니에요: 'am not / is not'. Coming next.

steps.q: Someone asks if you're a teacher. You're not. You say:

steps.note:
아니에요 = am not / is not.

학생이 아니에요.
→ I'm not a student.

Pattern: [noun]이/가 아니에요.
→ [noun] is not [it].

steps.title: 🔄 이에요 vs 아니에요
steps.text:
Positive: 저는 학생이에요. (I am a student.)
Negative: 저는 학생이 아니에요. (I am not a student.)

• 이에요/예요 = am / is
• 아니에요 = am not / is not

Notice: the negative uses 이/가 (subject marker) before 아니에요, not 은/는.
저는 학생이 아니에요 (topic: me, subject: student, not)

steps.q: 저는 학생이 아니에요 means:

steps.s: 저는 의사가 ___. (I am not a doctor.)

steps.note:
COMPOUND: 감사 (gratitude) + 합니다 (do-formally).
Literally: 'I do gratitude.'
Remember the sound change: 합니다 → [함니다].
That's nasalization from Hangul Stage 6!

Less formal than 감사합니다.
고맙습니다.
→ Thank you. (warmer, slightly less formal)
Use with people your age or younger. Still polite (-요 ending).

고맙습니다.
→ Thank you. (warmer, slightly less formal than 감사합니다)

steps.title: 🧱 Compound Pattern: noun + 합니다
steps.text:
You've now seen TWO words with this pattern:

• 감사 (gratitude) + 합니다 (do formally) = 감사합니다 (thank you)
• 안녕 (peace) + 하세요 (do, please) = 안녕하세요 (hello)

하다 = to do. The most common Korean verb.
When 하다 attaches to a noun, it creates a new verb.

감사하다
→ to thank (do-gratitude)

공부하다
→ to study (do-studying)

운동하다
→ to exercise (do-exercise)

You'll see this 하다 pattern hundreds of times.

deepDive.title: 하다 Verbs: The Biggest Pattern in Korean
deepDive.text:
About 40% of all Korean verbs follow this pattern:
noun + 하다 = verb

• 공부 (study) + 하다 = 공부하다 (to study)
• 운동 (exercise) + 하다 = 운동하다 (to exercise)
• 요리 (cooking) + 하다 = 요리하다 (to cook)
• 일 (work) + 하다 = 일하다 (to work)

Once you know the noun, the verb is free.
We'll teach these 하다 verbs throughout the course.

steps.q: 감사합니다 literally means:
You receive a gift from your friend's mother. You say:
steps.hint: Friend's MOTHER is an elder. Use the formal version.

steps.q: Your close friend helps you with homework. You say:
steps.hint: Close friend, same age. Casual-polite is fine.

steps.s: 'I am NOT a teacher' in Korean: 저는 선생님이 ___.

steps.q: 네 is often used in Korean conversation as a filler meaning:
steps.hint: Koreans say 네 constantly to show they're paying attention
No, I am NOT a student.

steps.q: Both 감사합니다 and 안녕하세요 share the same verb root. What is it?
steps.hint: 감사+합니다 and 안녕+하세요 both use 하다

steps.title: Three Ways to Say No
steps.desc: Korean has three negation tools. You already know 아니요 (standalone No) and 아니에요 (am/is not). Now meet the verb negation system. This is one of the most used structures in the language.

steps.note:
안 goes directly BEFORE the verb.

안 가요.
→ I'm not going. (I choose not to)

안 먹어요.
→ I don't eat it. (preference)

안 해요.
→ I'm not doing it.

⚡ Rule: 안 + verb = negation. That's it.

steps.title: 🚫 안 — The Simplest Negation
steps.text:
안 is a single syllable that goes BEFORE the verb.

안 가요.
→ Not going. (choice)

안 먹어요.
→ Don't eat. (preference)

안 마셔요.
→ Don't drink.

안 알아요.
→ Don't know.

안 괜찮아요.
→ Not okay.

⚡ LEGO: 안 + any verb = negative. One rule. Always works.

steps.q: 'I'm not going' (by choice):
steps.hint: 안 before the verb = not doing

steps.s: 이것은 커피___ 아니에요. (This is not coffee.)
steps.hint: 커피 ends in 이 (vowel) → 가 아니에요

steps.note:
Used for NOUNS: A는/은 B가/이 아니에요.

저는 학생이 아니에요.
→ I am not a student.

이것은 커피가 아니에요.
→ This is not coffee.

이것은 버스가 아니에요.
→ This is not a bus.

⚡ Note: 이/가 is used before 아니에요 — NOT 은/는.

steps.title: 🔄 이에요/예요 vs 아니에요
steps.text:
Positive → Negative:

저는 학생이에요.
→ I am a student.

저는 학생이 아니에요.
→ I am not a student.

이것은 커피예요.
→ This is coffee.

이것은 커피가 아니에요.
→ This is not coffee.

⚠️ The negative uses 이/가 before 아니에요:
학생이에요 → 학생이 아니에요 (same 이!)

steps.q: 'This is not a taxi':

steps.s: 저는 선생님___ 아니에요. (I'm not a teacher.)
steps.hint: 선생님 ends in ㅁ (consonant) → 이 아니에요

steps.note:
[verb stem] + 지 않아요

가지 않아요.
→ I do not go. (formal)

먹지 않아요.
→ I do not eat.

마시지 않아요.
→ I do not drink.

⚡ Same meaning as 안 + verb, but more formal.
안 가요 = 가지 않아요. Choose by register.

steps.title: 📊 Three Negation Tools — At a Glance
steps.text:
1. 아니요 — standalone 'no'
아니요, 괜찮아요.
→ No, I'm fine.

2. 아니에요 — '[noun] is not'
저는 학생이 아니에요.
→ I'm not a student.

3. 안 + [verb] — 'don't/not' (casual)
안 가요.
→ I'm not going.

4. [verb] + 지 않아요 — 'do not' (formal)
가지 않아요.
→ I do not go.

⚡ For now: 안 + verb for daily speech.
지 않아요 comes up in writing and formal talk.

steps.q: Which negation pattern is used for VERBS (not nouns)?
steps.hint: 아니에요 negates nouns (A is not B). 안 + verb negates actions.

steps.q: 'I don't eat spicy food (preference)':

steps.s: 저는 의사가 아니___. (I'm not a doctor.)
steps.hint: 아니에요 is the full form. 아니 + 에요

steps.q: 저는 학생이 아니에요 — what particle comes before 아니에요?
steps.hint: The noun before 아니에요 takes 이/가 (subject marker)

steps.title: 🇰🇷 Real Talk: When Do Koreans Use Each?
steps.text:
Daily conversation (90% of the time):
안 가요, 안 먹어요, 안 알아요.
→ Short, fast, natural.

Formal writing, presentations, polite emphasis:
가지 않아요, 먹지 않아요.
→ More explicit and considered.

For noun 'is not' — always:
아니에요 (no casual shortcut for this one)

⚡ Start with: 안 + verb for everything. You'll get 지 않아요 for free by Unit 7 when formal endings are taught.

steps.q: Same meaning as 안 먹어요:
steps.hint: 안 verb = verb + 지 않아요. Same meaning.

steps.title: What Is This?
steps.desc: Pointing at things and naming them. The most useful survival pattern: 이것은 뭐예요? (What is this?)

steps.note:
이 = this (near me). 것 = thing.

이것은 뭐예요?
→ As for this thing — what is it?

것 is one of the most common Korean words.

Short form of 무엇 (mu-eot). 뭐 is much more common in speech.

이것은 뭐예요?
→ What is this? (Survival question #1)

steps.q: 이것은 뭐예요? means:

steps.exampleEn: Is it coffee? Yes, that's right!
steps.note:
맞아요: 'is correct / that's right.'
Very useful confirmation word.
Use it to agree with someone.

steps.exampleEn: Is it a taxi? No, it's a bus.
steps.note:
아니요 = no (standalone rejection)
아니에요 = is not (negation inside sentence)

아니요, 학생이에요.
→ No, I'm a student. (correcting someone)

steps.q: Someone asks 이것은 뭐예요? pointing at your coffee. You say:

steps.title: 🗣️ Questions Without Question Words
steps.text:
In Korean, any statement becomes a question by raising your voice:

• 커피예요. (flat tone) = It's coffee.
• 커피예요? (rising tone) = Is it coffee?

No extra words needed. No word order change.
Just raise your intonation at the end.

This is why L1 said: Korean questions sound like statements.

steps.q: 커피예요? (with rising intonation) means:
커피예요? 네, 맞아요! What does 맞아요 mean?

steps.note:
이거 = casual short form of 이것.
Much more common in daily speech.
이거 뭐예요? = What's this? (casual but polite)

steps.q: 이거 is the short form of:

steps.hint: 컴퓨터 ends in ㅓ (vowel). Vowel → 예요
호텔 ends in ㄹ batchim (consonant). Consonant → 이에요

steps.q: Someone shows you a photo. You point and ask what it is. You say:
이것은 인터넷이에요? 아니요, 컴퓨터예요. What was the answer?
steps.hint: 아니요 = no. Then the correction: 컴퓨터예요.

steps.q: 이것은 커피예요? 네, ___!

steps.title: Nationality. Country Names Are Compounds
steps.desc: Korean words are built from reusable building blocks. Each block has a meaning. Snap them together and you can decode thousands of words. This lesson: country names.
goals: See how Korean builds words like LEGO

steps.title: 🏷️ Korean Names: Family Name First
steps.text:
Korean names: FAMILY NAME + given name.

• 김민수 = Kim (family) + Minsu (given)
• 3 most common family names: 김, 이, 박 (Kim, Lee, Park)
• These 3 cover about 45% of all Koreans!
• NEVER call someone by their given name unless you're close
• Use title + family name: 김 선생님 (Teacher Kim)
• Or just the title: 선생님! (Teacher!)

deepDive.title: Korean Name Culture
deepDive.text:
Only about 300 family names exist in Korea
(versus thousands in English).

Given names are usually 2 syllables,
often with Chinese-origin meaning.

Married women keep their family name.

Calling someone by first name (이름)
without permission is RUDE.

Even couples often use titles early in relationships.

At work: 김 대리님 (Manager Kim),
박 과장님 (Section Chief Park).

The word 이름 appears in 이름이 뭐예요?
but Koreans rarely use first names in daily life.

steps.title: 🧬 Sino-Korean: Your Secret Weapon
steps.text:
About 60% of Korean words are built from small meaning-blocks that originally came from Chinese.

You do NOT need to learn Chinese.
You do NOT need to memorize characters.

All you need is the MEANING of each block:

국 means 'country'
학 means 'study'
생 means 'person/life'

Snap them together:
학 (study) + 생 (person) → 학생 (student)

One block appears in dozens of words. Learn 50 blocks, decode 500+ words. This is the #1 accelerator for Korean vocabulary.

deepDive.title: Why does Korean have Chinese-origin words?
deepDive.text:
For over a thousand years, Korea used Chinese characters for ALL writing. Korean scholars, government officials, and writers wrote exclusively in Classical Chinese.

The problem: Chinese characters are incredibly difficult to learn. Only the educated elite could read and write. Most Korean people were illiterate.

In 1443, King Sejong the Great invented Hangul specifically so that ordinary people could read and write. He said: 'A wise person can learn it in a morning, a fool in ten days.'

But even after Hangul was created, the Chinese-origin VOCABULARY stayed. Korean kept thousands of Chinese-root words, just spelled them in Hangul instead of Chinese characters.

This is why 60% of Korean words are 'Sino-Korean': the SOUNDS and MEANINGS come from Chinese, but the WRITING is pure Hangul.

steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN COMPOUND:
한 (Korea) + 국 (country).
국 appears in ALL country names.
Learn this block. It unlocks every country word.

SINO-KOREAN: 미 (beautiful) + 국 (country).
'Beautiful country': the old Chinese name.
Same 국 as 한국!

SINO-KOREAN: 일 (sun) + 본 (origin).
'Sun origin': Land of the Rising Sun.

SINO-KOREAN: 중 (center) + 국 (country).
'Center country': Middle Kingdom.
Same 국 again!

Native Korean word (not Sino-Korean).
[country] + 사람: [nationality] person.
Simple, consistent pattern.

steps.q: What does the 국 in 한국 mean?
steps.hint: 국 = country. It appears in all country names.

steps.q: 미국 literally means:

steps.title: 🧱 국 = Country (LEGO Piece #1)
steps.text:
You just learned your first reusable Sino-Korean morpheme:

국 = country

한국 = Korea (Korean country)
미국 = America (beautiful country)
중국 = China (center country)
외국 = foreign country (외 = outside)

ONE piece, many words. Korean is LEGO.

steps.s: 저는 ___ 사람이에요. (I am a Korean person.)
저는 미국 ___이에요. (I am an American person.)

steps.q: How do you say 'I am Japanese'?
Korean names put the family name:
steps.hint: Family name FIRST: 김민수 = Kim (family) + Minsu (given)

steps.q: Which three family names cover about 45% of all Koreans?
steps.hint: Kim, Lee, Park are the big three

steps.s: 일본 사람이에요? 아니요, ___ 사람이에요. (No, I'm Chinese.)

steps.q: 중국 = 중 + 국. What does 중 mean?

steps.title: Nice to Meet You
steps.desc: The full meeting phrase, plus essential expressions you'll use every day. By the end of this lesson, you can handle a basic first meeting.

steps.note:
Fixed phrase. Memorize as a chunk.
Used in first meetings. Formal and warm.
Literally: 'Having met you, I'm glad.'

이름: name. 이: subject marker.
뭐예요: what is?
Answer: 저는 [name]이에요/예요.

steps.q: 이름이 뭐예요? means:

steps.note:
Very formal apology. Use with strangers, elders.
죄송 (apology) + 합니다 (do formally).
Same structure as 감사합니다!

steps.exampleEn: Just a moment! I'm coming now.
steps.note:
잠시: a moment. 만: only. 요: polite.
Use when you need someone to wait.
Very common in shops, restaurants, offices.

Two uses:
1. 'It's okay' (responding to an apology)
2. 'I'm fine' (when asked how you are)
3. 'No thanks' (declining politely)

steps.q: Someone introduces themselves. You respond:
You accidentally bump into someone on the subway. You say:

steps.title: 🤝 The Korean First Meeting Sequence
steps.text:
Koreans follow a predictable pattern when meeting:

1. 안녕하세요!
→ Hello!

2. 저는 [이름]이에요/예요.
→ I am [name].

3. 만나서 반갑습니다!
→ Nice to meet you!

Optional follow-ups:
4. 이름이 뭐예요?
→ What's your name?

5. 어디 사람이에요?
→ Where are you from?

Bow slightly when you say 만나서 반갑습니다 — a small nod is enough.

steps.q: Someone says 죄송합니다 after stepping on your foot. You say:
A waiter says 잠시만요 when you enter a restaurant. This means:
죄송합니다 uses the same compound pattern as 감사합니다. Both use:

steps.s: You need the waiter to wait. You say: ___

steps.q: How does 괜찮아요 function when offered food you don't want?
Put the first meeting in order: A) 만나서 반갑습니다 B) 안녕하세요 C) 저는 민수예요
steps.hint: Hello → I am [name] → Nice to meet you

steps.title: Words That Trick You
steps.desc: Some Konglish words SOUND like English but mean something DIFFERENT. These false friends will trip you up if you're not warned.

steps.exampleEn: This is on the house / a freebie.
steps.note:
⚠️ FALSE FRIEND: Sounds like 'service'
Actual meaning: FREE EXTRA at restaurants.
서비스로 드릴게요: 'I'll give it as a freebie.'
NOT related to customer service!

⚠️ HEADS UP: 'Handphone' is Konglish.
English speakers say 'cellphone' or 'mobile.'
Koreans won't understand 'cellphone.'
핸드폰 or 스마트폰: use these.

steps.exampleEn: I have a part-time job today.
steps.note:
⚠️ FALSE FRIEND: NOT 'byte' (computer)!
From German 'Arbeit' (work).
바이트하다: to work part-time.
Also written 아르바이트 (a-reu-ba-i-teu).

steps.q: A restaurant gives you extra side dishes saying '서비스예요.' This means:
바이트 in Korean means:

steps.en: You can do it! / Go for it!
steps.note:
⚠️ HEADS UP: Sounds like 'fighting'
but means ENCOURAGEMENT, not violence.
Koreans say 파이팅! to cheer you on.
One of the most beloved Korean expressions.

Shortened Konglish: 'air con(ditioner)'
Koreans clip long English words:
air conditioner → 에어컨
remote control → 리모컨
digital camera → 디카

steps.q: In Korea, a cellphone is called:
Your Korean friend says 파이팅! before your job interview. They mean:

steps.title: ✂️ Koreans Clip English Words
steps.text:
Korean often shortens long English words:

• air conditioner → 에어컨 (e-eo-keon)
• remote control → 리모컨 (ri-mo-keon)
• digital camera → 디카 (di-ka)
• apartment → 아파트 (a-pa-teu)
• convenience store → 편의점 (not Konglish!)

This clipping happens because Korean prefers 2-3 syllable words.
Long Konglish gets trimmed down.

steps.s: 오늘 ___가 있어요. (I have a part-time job today.)

steps.q: 에어컨 is short for:
At a Korean restaurant, the owner brings an extra dish and says '서비스예요!' What should you do?

steps.title: 🌏 Why False Friends Exist
steps.text:
False friends happen because Korean borrowed the English SOUND but changed the MEANING.

English does this too:
• 'Angst' in English means worry (German: deep existential dread)
• 'Kindergarten' in English means preschool (German: literally 'children garden')

Every language reshapes borrowed words.
Korean is no different. Just be aware of the traps!

steps.q: Which of these is NOT a false friend (meaning matches English)?
steps.hint: 커피 actually means coffee. The others have shifted meanings.

steps.title: Your First Korean Conversation
steps.desc: Putting it all together: a real first-meeting dialogue. Plus the most important cultural note of the entire course: 나이 (age).
goals: Know why Koreans ask your age

steps.note:
🌍 CULTURAL KEY: In Korea, age determines
EVERYTHING: who bows deeper, who pours
drinks, which speech level you use.
Koreans ask age early. It's NOT rude.
It's NECESSARY to know how to speak to you.

steps.title: 🎌 나이: Why Koreans Ask Your Age
steps.text:
In English, asking someone's age is often rude.
In Korean, it's ESSENTIAL.

Age determines:
• Speech level (polite, formal, casual)
• Who bows deeper
• Who pours the drink
• Titles (형/오빠/누나/언니)

If a Korean asks your age within 5 minutes of meeting you, they're being POLITE. They want to speak to you correctly.

deepDive.title: The Korean Age Hierarchy
deepDive.text:
Korean has special titles based on age and gender:

형 (hyeong): older brother (said by males)
오빠 (oppa): older brother (said by females)
누나 (nuna): older sister (said by males)
언니 (eonni): older sister (said by females)

These are used for ANY older person you're close to,
not just real siblings.

A Korean born in 1990 speaking to someone born in 1988:
the 1990-born person speaks with more respect.
Two years difference changes everything.

Koreans calculate age differently too.
Traditional Korean age: you're 1 at birth and gain
a year every January 1st. Korea recently switched
to international age for legal purposes (2023).

steps.q: A Korean asks your age upon first meeting. This is:
opts: Normal and polite: they need it for speech levels
steps.ans: Normal and polite: they need it for speech levels
steps.hint: Age determines how Koreans speak to each other

steps.exampleEn: Yes, I'm also pleased to meet you!
steps.note:
저: I (polite). 도: also/too (particle).
저 + 도 = me too.
도 just attaches to words. Simple.

steps.exampleEn: Where are you from? (lit: where person are you?)
steps.note:
어디: where.
어디 사람이에요? = Where person are you?
This means 'Where are you from?'

steps.en: Please take care of me / Please be good to me
steps.exampleEn: Please take care of me going forward.
steps.note:
Common closing phrase in first meetings.
잘: well. 부탁: request. 합니다: do (formal).
Used at work, school, any new relationship.

steps.q: 저도 means:

steps.s: 안녕하세요! 저는 민수예요. 만나서 반갑습니다! 앞으로 잘 ___.
steps.hint: Please take care of me (formal closing)

steps.title: 💬 Full First Meeting Script
steps.text:
Here's a natural Korean first meeting:

A: 안녕하세요! (Hello!)
B: 안녕하세요! (Hello!)
A: 저는 사라예요. 이름이 뭐예요? (I'm Sara. What's your name?)
B: 저는 민수예요. 만나서 반갑습니다! (I'm Minsu. Nice to meet you!)
A: 저도 반갑습니다! (I'm also pleased to meet you!)

You know EVERY word in this dialogue.

steps.q: 안녕하세요! 저는 민수예요. What did this person just do?
어디 사람이에요? means:

steps.s: 네, ___반갑습니다! (Yes, I'm also pleased to meet you!)

steps.q: When do Koreans typically say 잘 부탁합니다?
steps.hint: It's a closing phrase for first meetings

steps.q: The particle 도 in 저도 means:
steps.hint: 도 = also/too. It attaches to nouns.

steps.q: Put the meeting phrases in natural order: A) 잘 부탁합니다 B) 안녕하세요 C) 만나서 반갑습니다 D) 저는 [name]이에요
steps.hint: Hello → I am [name] → Nice to meet you → Please take care of me

steps.s: 저는 미국 사람이에요. ___사람이에요? (Where are you from?)

steps.title: Unit 1 Review
steps.desc: Let's check what you've learned. All questions use ONLY vocabulary from this unit. No surprises.
goals: Prove you know the goodbye rules

steps.q: How do you say 'hello' in Korean?
안녕하세요 decomposes into:
감사합니다 decomposes into:

steps.s: You're leaving a Korean home. The host stays. You say: ___
steps.hint: Host STAYS. Wish them to 'stay in peace.'

steps.q: What does 서비스 ACTUALLY mean in Korean?
한국 = 한 + 국. What does 국 mean?

steps.hint: 학생 ends in a consonant. Consonant → 이에요

steps.q: 'Goodbye' to someone leaving:
In Korean, the verb goes:
steps.hint: Korean is SOV. Verb last, always.

steps.q: 이것은 뭐예요? means:
저는 의사가 아니에요. This means:
opts: I want to be a doctor

steps.s: 미국 사람이에요? 아니요, ___ 사람이에요. (No, I'm Korean.)

steps.q: Korean has how many speech levels?
steps.hint: You learned this in the 해요체 tip

steps.q: In the dictionary, all Korean verbs end in:

steps.s: Someone bumps into you and says 죄송합니다. You say: ___

steps.q: Korean names put the family name:
steps.hint: Family name FIRST: 김민수 = Kim Minsu

steps.q: A Korean asks 나이가 어떻게 되세요? within 5 minutes. They're being:
opts: Polite: they need your age for speech levels
steps.ans: Polite: they need your age for speech levels
steps.hint: Age determines everything in Korean social interaction
